caption: At the CARE Network Summit and Correctional Practice and Research Symposium 2025, close to 500 partners came together to chart the next chapter of rehabilitation and reintegration in Singapore. The summit saw the launch of the CARE Network's fiveyear workplan and featured practicebased research sessions that will inform future interventions. Our community partners' voices remind us that rehabilitation is a collective journey built on trust, shared experience and enduring hope. caption: How can we work together to support our youths, so that they can be the best versions of themselves? This year, as part of SG60, SPS partnered and to engage and bring new programmes for reformative trainees at Institution Tanah Merah. and conducted a series of workshops where reformative trainees worked with mentors to plan for life after their release, and the actions they can take to achieve them. SG60 is about Building Our Singapore Together, and how all of us can contribute to a Singapore that we will proudly call home. This initiative will also contribute to the SG Youth Plan - a five-year action plan created by youths, for youths, and supported by the whole of society. SPS SingaporePrisonService CaptainsOfLives SG4SecondChances BeyondPrisonWalls BehindPrisonWalls FromStereotypestoArchetypes AOLSG MCCY nycsg SG60 SGYouthPlan

caption: Driving Innovation, Owning Innovation! On 27 March 2025, 11 teams of CaptainsOfLives showcased their creative AI-driven ideas at the Finals of the iDare Make-A-Pitch AIdeathon 2025. Congratulations to Team FIFA for securing first place with Digital Photo Submission with AI Moderationan efficient system that streamlines photo screening, supports families, and frees up staff for critical tasks! Team CEMS placed second for their Community-Based Programmes (CBP) Emplacement Management System (CEMS), an automated solution ensuring accurate and efficient CBP data consolidation. Team Innova took the third place with Clear & Earn Hours Automation, streamlining work hour management, while enhancing transparency and accountability.But the real win? The passion and innovation displayed by every SPS staff who stepped up to find innovative ways and leverage technology to simplify work processes, so that they may focus on higher order work! A huge thank you to our esteemed judgesMr Ng Pan Yong, Chief Innovation Officer, ; SAC Rockey Francisco Junior, Deputy Commissioner (Policy & Transformation); and DAC Patricia Tung, Director (Strategic Planning Division)! SPS SingaporePrisonService SG4SecondChances BehindPrisonWalls idare2025

caption: lovewins youareenoughThis March 8th, International Womens Day, I was invited to speak at Changi Female Prison. I brought my mum, making her dream come trueto return not as a prisoner, but as a beacon of hope. Mum was incarcerated in Changi from the mid-70s to 1983 for drug offenses. She turned her life around, living drug-free for decades. Her presence that day was a powerful message: *Its never too late to change. You are enough.* The inmates asked raw, valid questions about change and forgiveness. Mums life was the answerproof that no matter how far you fall, you can rise again. This day also reminded me of my familys 30-year journey to reconciliation. My parents divorced, and Dad remarried, but by Gods grace and effort, weve built a close-knit family. I adore my step-siblings, Adam and Andrea, and cherish the love we share. Ive been blessed with an incredible support systemmy overprotective brother, Spencer, who stepped up as a parent, my amazing aunties, uncles, and my Granny, who taught me, *Nothing is impossible, and family is worth fighting for.* To the ladies at Changi: Keep the faith. Believe in yourselves. Your past doesnt define your future. Never give up. And to anyone reading: Family and love are worth fighting for. Healing is possible. Grateful to AGAPE and Changi Prison for this meaningful opportunity. Forgiveness Reconciliation SecondChances Hope Family NeverGiveUp LoveWins

I brought my mum, making her dream come true to return not as a prisoner, but as a beacon of hope. Mum was incarcerated in Changi from the mid-70s to 1983 for drug offenses. She turned her life around, living drug-free for decades. Her presence that day was a powerful message: *It's never too late to change. You are enough.* The inmates asked raw, valid questions about change and forgiveness. Mum's life was the answer proof that no matter how far you fall, you can rise again. This day also reminded me of my family's 30-year journey to reconciliation. My parents divorced, and my dad remarried, but by God's grace and effort, we've built a close-knit family. I've been blessed with an incredible support system—my overprotective brother, who stepped up as a parent, my amazing aunties, uncles, and my Granny, who taught me, *Nothing is impossible, and family is worth fighting for.* To the ladies at Changi: Keep the faith. Believe in yourselves. Your past doesn’t define your future. Never give up. And to anyone reading: Family and love are worth fighting for. Healing is possible. Grateful to AGAPE and Changi Prison for this meaningful opportunity. Forgiveness Reconciliation SecondChances Hope Family NeverGiveUp LoveWins

caption: Stepping into the Future with Corrections 2030. The SPS-YRSG Corporate Advance 2024, themed Forward Corrections: Beyond Second Chances, was held on 30 April 2024 at the Singapore EXPO. This years annual seminar saw about 600 SPS and staff and strategic partners come together to share future plans for the next bound of transformation in corrections. Guest-of-Honour, Minister for Home Affairs and Minister for Law, Mr K Shanmugam, commended SPSs relentless efforts towards rehabilitation and reintegration, which have led to remarkable two-year and five-year recidivism rates. As we transition from Corrections 2025 to Corrections 2030, were charting new territories with: S.M.A.R.T. Prisons Uplifting and Strengthening inmates familiesMaximising the employability and career progression of inmates and ex-offenders Mobilising the Community Developing our CaptainsOfLives Together, we will continue to work towards a safer, and more inclusive Singapore. caption: Meet CW Ragini D/O Anpazhahan, a Correctional Unit Officer with the Singapore Prison Service (SPS)! CW Ragini has always been interested to work in a uniformed organisation. She joined SPS in 2014 after some encouragement from her family. Having been with SPS for nine years, CW Ragini has taken on many concurrent roles, including being a Looking Forward facilitator and trainer. CW Ragini is also one of the key members responsible for the successful implementation of the Arts Behind Bars programme in Institution A4, which uses art as a form of rehabilitation for women inmates. This year, crafts by the women inmates will be exhibited in Vienna during the 66th Session of the UN Commission on Narcotics Drugs from 13 to 17 March 2023, under a section titled Arts for Women. The exhibition emphasises the importance of strong community support in helping drug abusers build strong family units and live drug-free and productive lives. CW Ragini's story is a testament to the many contributions that women make in the field of corrections. As a woman CaptainOfLives (COLs), she has positively impacted the lives of inmates under her charge. As we celebrate International Women's Day today, CW Ragini would like to share a simple but powerful quote from Michelle Obama, "There is no limit to what we, as women, can accomplish." InternationalWomensDay BehindPrisonWalls BeyondPrisonWalls SG4SecondChances JoinUS

caption: On 23 February 2023, 23 inmate students proudly received their newly attained NITEC in Business Services certificates during their Graduation ceremony in Changi Prison Complex.Launched in 2021, the NITEC programme for inmates is a collaboration between the Singapore Prison Service (SPS), Yellow Ribbon Singapore (YRSG) and the Institute of Technical Education (ITE) to offer inmates the opportunity to upgrade their education while serving their sentence in prison. The Business Services Programme covers six modules: Business Communication Introduction to Data Analytics & Cyber-Security Social Media Marketing Customer Service Event Planning & Administration Sales Technique & Engagement. Amongst the inmate students who graduated from the programme is Lily (not her real name), who was incarcerated for cheating-related offences. Despite initially hesitating to apply for the programme, Lily has since demonstrated a commitment to upgrade her education and took advantage of the educational opportunities available.With better qualifications, inmates have more employment opportunities upon their release to ease their reintegration into society! CaptainsOfLives BeyondSecondChances BeyondPrisonWalls SG4SecondChances

caption: DidYouKnow? Just like students in mainstream schools, inmate students from the Prison School take their national examinations during the same period as them nationwide. On 17 February 2023, 16 inmate students received their GCE 'A' Level examination results. Among the 16 students who received their results was 'Jake', who is serving a 14-year sentence for drug-related offences. Motivated by the success stories of fellow ex-offenders, 'Jake' decided to pursue his education behind bars. The 29-year-old shared, "I never enjoyed studying, but I found the strength to persevere because of my parents' support. They always told me the results do not matter as long as I did my best. It was stressful, but I coped by playing basketball and sharing jokes with other inmates." 'Jake' was pleasantly surprised that he scored a 'C' for his weakest subject, H1 Mathematics, contrary to the 'F' he received during his preliminary examinations.Mr Tan Teck Khiang, a Principles of Accounts (POA) teacher at Prison School, would often go the extra mile for 'Jake' and other inmate students by preparing additional teaching materials to supplement their lesson plans. He hopes that all inmate students continue to do their best after release and apply the accounting principles taught in their daily lives. CaptainsOfLives BehindPrisonWalls BeyondPrisonWalls SG4SecondChances JoinUs

caption: Introducing our CaptainsOfLives couple, CW Mu Weiqiang Cleve and CW Chen Lifen Joann!A chance meeting at the lift of their housing estate blossomed into a happy marriage of over 12 years and counting! Our first date was quite boring , as CW Chen recounted. I was still a trainee at the Singapore Prison Training Institute. When Cleve asked me out, we went on a study date at the Changi Airport. And he gave me some advice to help me with my upcoming test!Today, CW Chen is a Correctional Unit Officer overseeing inmates' well-being and rehabilitation programmes in her Correctional Unit at Institution A4.CW Mu, an officer from the Singapore Prisons Emergency Action Response (SPEAR) Force, is the Officer-in-Charge of the Prison Secure Ward, where he oversees the security and administration of the ward.On what its like to have a spouse who works in the same organisation, CW Mu reflects, It's great because were able to consult one another about our daily issues and challenges since we both know the work culture. At the end of our workday, well always make time to exercise together and then have dinner!Whats the secret behind their everlasting love? Appreciate your partners work ethic. Give your partner space when needed but always be thankful for their presence.Happy Valentines Day from the Captains of Lives! BeyondPrisonWalls BehindPrisonWalls SG4SecondChances HappyValentinesDay
cleaned_caption: Introducing our CaptainsOfLives couple! A chance meeting at the lift of their housing estate blossomed into a happy marriage of over 12 years and counting! Our first date was quite boring, as one of them recounted. I was still a trainee at the Singapore Prison Training Institute. When my partner asked me out, we went on a study date at Changi Airport. And he gave me some advice to help me with my upcoming test! Today, one is a Correctional Unit Officer overseeing inmates' well-being and rehabilitation programmes in her Correctional Unit at Institution A4. The other is an officer from the Singapore Prisons Emergency Action Response (SPEAR) Force, serving as the Officer-in-Charge of the Prison Secure Ward, where he oversees the security and administration of the ward. On what it's like to have a spouse who works in the same organisation, he reflects, it's great because we're able to consult one another about our daily issues and challenges since we both know the work culture. At the end of our workday, we'll always make time to exercise together and then have dinner! What's the secret behind their everlasting love? Appreciate your partner's work ethic. Give your partner space when needed but always be thankful for their presence. Happy Valentine's Day from the Captains of Lives!

caption: Seven incarcerated fathers reunited with their children on 23 December 2022 during a family-joint session in Changi Prison Complex. The session was facilitated by Singapore Prison Service and Awful Grace, a non-profit organisation. The programme, themed 'Dads Do Care', aims to help inmates maintain and improve their relationships with their children through parent-child bonding activities, such as pottery. At the end of the session, the children brought the pottery artwork home as a memento. With parental support, children of incarcerated parents can avoid developing insecurities and anti-social behaviours. As such, sessions like 'Dads Do Care' help prevent intergenerational offending and decrease the likelihood for inmates to reoffend. CaptainsOfLives BehindPrisonWalls BeyondPrisonWalls

caption: , . ASP Ng RiyingAfter working in the private sector for three years, ASP Ng joined the Singapore Prison Service (SPS) in 2009, in search of a meaningful career which could help make a difference in someones life.Upon the completion of her training, ASP Ng was assigned to work at the former Changi Womens Prison/Drug Rehabilitation Centre (CWP). It was there she learnt that she needed to be able to adapt quickly to respond to any situation as part of her duties as a prison officer.She recalls one incident where she had to separate an inmate from her cellmates after a heated argument. When the inmate calmed down, ASP Ng found her in tears. She took the time to speak with the inmate, who had from then on, opened up to ASP Ng about her issues. The day before her release, she thanked ASP Ng for her encouragement, which helped her change for the better. Since then, ASP Ng has felt a tremendous sense of pride in her role as a prison officer, which has allowed her to inspire change in the lives of others. , ASP Ng has also been practising Muay Thai for nearly four years. Besides keeping fit, Muay Thai also sharpens her focus and concentration, when learning the techniques in class.ASP Ng reflects, Muay Thai has taught me to remain calm and resilient when facing challenges. These are useful traits to have as CaptainsOfLives, as we need to adapt to change and sometimes make decisions quickly.BehindPrisonWalls BeyondPrisonWalls

Upon the completion of her training, ASP Ng was assigned to work at the former Changi Women's Prison/Drug Rehabilitation Centre (CWP). It was there she learnt that she needed to be able to adapt quickly to respond to any situation as part of her duties as a prison officer. She recalls one incident where she had to separate an inmate from her cellmates after a heated argument. When the inmate calmed down, ASP Ng found her in tears. She took the time to speak with the inmate, who had from then on, opened up to ASP Ng about her issues. The day before her release, she thanked ASP Ng for her encouragement, which helped her change for the better. Since then, ASP Ng has felt a tremendous sense of pride in her role as a prison officer, which has allowed her to inspire change in the lives of others. ASP Ng has also been practising Muay Thai for nearly four years. Besides keeping fit, Muay Thai also sharpens her focus and concentration, when learning the techniques in class. ASP Ng reflects, Muay Thai has taught me to remain calm and resilient when facing challenges. These are useful traits to have as Captains of Lives, as we need to adapt to change and sometimes make decisions quickly. Behind Prison Walls Beyond Prison Walls

caption: Our inaugural batch of 17 inmates have graduated from the TAP (Train and Place) & Grow Precision Engineering Training Programme!They received their Workforce Skills Qualifications (WSQ) Advanced Certificate in Precision Engineering from Assoc Prof Muhammad Faishal Ibrahim , Minister of State, Ministry of Home Affairs and Ministry of National Development during a graduation ceremony on 27 April 2022.The programme, a collaboration that started in 2020 between , Precision Engineering and Technology Association and Nanyang Polytechnic , is part of YRSG's TAP & Grow initiative to partner like-minded employers, training institutions, and community partners to set up training academies in the prisons. caption: . , . SGT Zulhilmi The search for a meaningful career led SGT Zulhilmi to the Singapore Prison Service in 2018. As a Personal Supervisor at the Reformative Training Centre, his duties include enforcing the safety and security of inmates, as well as meaningfully engage those under his care, most of whom are young offenders. , Zulhilmi is a budding musician who writes and composes his own music. This multi-instrumentalists passion for songwriting began in his early school years, where he taught himself to play the drums, guitar and keyboard. Recently, SGT Zulhilmi was able to showcase his musical talents when he joined Mediacorp Surias songwriting competition . Putting all his skills to highlight a serious social issue, Zulhilmi wrote a song to motivate women victims of domestic abuse. For his moving composition, he was awarded first runner-up for the competition. I feel grateful that I had the opportunity and support from my family, friends and colleagues, Zulhilmi reflected. What I love about music is its ability to connect people together and shed light on important issues. CaptainsOfLives BehindPrisonWalls BeyondPrisonWalls BeyondTheUniform

caption: We started in the same squad back in 1991. Both of us are very active in sports. We used to play badminton and hang out together after work.What started out as a friendship soon blossomed into a fulfilling marriage and partnership that has lasted over 30 years for our CaptainsOfLives, RO Charlvi Tan and ASP Toh Siew Choo.Suddenly, something changed, and I held her hand one day while crossing the street and here we are 30 years later, RO Tan recalled. Its definitely been an adventure, ASP Toh said. The good thing about being married to another Prison Officer is that you have someone who understands what youre going through. Were able to share our experiences, learn from each other, share our problems and come up with solutions together. Rising through the ranks together, the couple, who started as Junior Prison Officers and are now Senior Prison Officers, is grateful for the opportunities with the Singapore Prison Service (SPS).It feels nice to be recognised for your efforts, the father of two said. We always attend each others promotion ceremony to the point that our colleagues joke about meeting us at all ceremonies! Today, ASP Toh, is the OC Medical Centre of Institution A4, where she oversees and manages the prison operations of a Medical Centre in Institution A4. RO Tan is a Housing Unit Officer at Institution B2, where he oversees the daily operations in the housing unit with inmates pending Courts sentencing and instill order and discipline among them. Together with their team members, both ASP Toh and RO Tan always look for opportunities to motivate the inmates in their rehabilitation journey through the engagements during their daily prison operations.Whats the secret behind their everlasting love? Understanding and learning to trust one another is key, you must also give each other their personal space, moral support and lots of encouragement. Happy Valentines Day! BehindPrisonWalls BeyondPrisonWalls HappyValentinesDay

caption: Meet ASP Muhammad Ali Mochtar our CaptainOfLives and co-star of the recently released three-part SPS series. Ali is featured in the second part titled Step Forward and he shared his debut film experience with us! () (), ?It was definitely a good experience being able to share about my job with the cast and crew during filming.In real life, Im a Manager for Community Corrections (Halfway House/Home Detention) at the Community Corrections Command (COMC) in the SPS. I work with a team of ROs to oversee supervisees who are undergoing the Community-Based Programme (CBP) at various community facilities. What was featured in the film was just a glimpse of our work. We work with multiple stakeholders, such as Correctional Rehabilitation Specialists (CRS), employers, case management partners, as well as the families of supervisees. For complex cases, we conduct home visits, as well as joint sessions with family, or employers. ?Some people think that we only monitor the discipline of supervisees who are serving their sentences in the community. However, theres much more to it! ROs also provide prosocial support that aids the supervisees in their rehabilitation and reintegration. -?By giving ex-offenders second chances, we help to break their cycle of re-offending. If you know of any ex-offenders, be it your co-worker, or your neighbour, show your support and encourage them to be better individuals. You can be their agents of change!BeyondPrisonWalls BehindPrisonWalls StepForward

caption: Mr Bala Muraly retired earlier this year on 21 April 2021 after an illustrious 29-year career with the Singapore Prison Service (SPS) as a former Senior Reintegration Officer. For Mr Bala, his years as a CaptainOfLives were some of the best years of his life because he was able to improve the lives of the inmates and ex-offenders that he supervised. Despite having retired, Mr Bala continues to keep in touch with his former SPS colleagues. The apple does not fall too far from the tree Mr Balas passion is echoed by his daughter, Ms Previtha Jay. She decided to follow her fathers footsteps and joined SPS. She is currently a Senior Prison Officer trainee and undergoing the Prison Officers Course. Ms Jay, who always wanted to become a uniform officer, grew up listening to inspirational stories from her father on how Prison Officers contribute in rehabilitating and improving inmates lives. She feels lucky to have her father by her side to guide her as she sets out to chart her own legacy as a CaptainOfLives. PressPlay and tune in to our rendition of The Road Ahead, a collaboration between Music and SPS, which features Mr Bala alongside our SPS staff, community partners and beneficiaries as part of commemorating SPS 75th anniversary! SPS75 TransformingLivesWithYou SPSHeritage CaptainsOfLives

caption: : Henry (not his real name) was caught and imprisoned in his late 20s for participating in gang related activities. Now at 30, he is focused on becoming a better role model to his two-year-old daughter and a responsible husband to his wife. To do this, Henry decided to proactively participate in several psychology-based correctional programmes in prison.Some of these programmes included the Social Skills Training Programme, which is designed to help inmates improve their communication and relational skills with their loved ones, and Thinking for Change Programme which is developed to raise ones awareness and target thinking patterns that led to anti-social and criminal activity behaviours.This year, Henry decided to participate in the National Youth Achievement Award (NYAA) programme. Together, with the support from SPS, community partners and his peers, he crafted handmade cards as part of a collaboration between SPS and a local non-profit organisation Be Kind SG.Henrys Personal Supervisor, RO Gwan Wei Lin recalls, When I first met Henry, he was quiet and likes to keep to himself. After encouraging him to join the NYAA programme, Ive seen a lot of positive change in him. He knows what he wants for his future and has also developed leadership skills through the programme.Henry also actively participated in the 3M Move @ South East 2021 initiative, where for every 20 minutes of exercise he performed, the South East Community Development Council would donate one 3M product to the underprivileged. I found the sporting activities during NYAA Programme meaningful because I can relate to them. From such activities, I also learnt about life skills such as teamwork and better communication. I will definitely continue to carry these lessons with me, even after I leave prison! - HenryNYAA2021 BehindPrisonWalls SecondChances

Some of these programmes included the Social Skills Training Programme, which is designed to help inmates improve their communication and relational skills with their loved ones, and the Thinking for Change Programme, which is developed to raise awareness and target thinking patterns that led to anti-social and criminal activity behaviours. This year, he decided to participate in the National Youth Achievement Award (NYAA) programme. Together, with the support from Singapore Prison Service, community partners, and his peers, he crafted handmade cards as part of a collaboration between Singapore Prison Service and a local non-profit organisation Be Kind SG. 

His Personal Supervisor recalls, "When I first met him, he was quiet and liked to keep to himself. After encouraging him to join the NYAA programme, I've seen a lot of positive change in him. He knows what he wants for his future and has also developed leadership skills through the programme." He also actively participated in the 3M Move @ South East 2021 initiative, where for every 20 minutes of exercise he performed, the South East Community Development Council would donate one 3M product to the underprivileged. "I found the sporting activities during the NYAA Programme meaningful because I can relate to them. From such activities, I also learnt about life skills such as teamwork and better communication. I will definitely continue to carry these lessons with me, even after I leave prison!" 

#NYAA2021 #BehindPrisonWalls #SecondChances

caption: The National Youth Achievement Award (NYAA) 2021 Ceremony was held on 17 November 2021 at the Prison School and concurrently at Cluster A and B to comply strictly with the safe management measures. There were 33 Gold, 32 Silver and 17 Bronze awards given to the inmate participants.The theme for NYAA 2021 was STEP Up to Reach Out, highlighting participants accomplishments in four areas - Service to Community, To Aspire and Dream, Excel through Learning, and Pave Your Own Path.Guest-of-Honour Mr Chan Chun Sing, Minister for Education, commended the efforts of inmate participants in demonstrating their resilience to complete the NYAA programme in the face of adversity, as well as the dedication of SPS, the NYAA council and partners who persevered during the COVID-19 pandemic to provide opportunities for inmate participants to make a positive impact to society while still serving their sentences in prisons.BehindPrisonWalls CaptainsOfLives

caption: [ 3 8 ] The Jamiyah Halfway House, one of our community partners, works closely with us to ensure successful rehabilitation and reintegration of offenders as theyre completing their sentences. Family support is everything, explained Faizal Abas, Head of Jamiyah Halfway House (JHH). Thats why some residents are also granted overnight home leave to spend time with their family during Hari Raya.During these festive occasions, residents may also create artworks under JHHs Art Therapy rehabilitative programme and send them as well-wishes to their loved ones. These small gestures go a long way in helping them rebuild their relationships with their families.These are just some of the rehabilitation programmes available for JHH residents. Find out more about the programmes in the photos. BeyondPrisonWalls

caption: The Selarang Halfway House (SHWH) was established by the Singapore Prison Service (SPS) in 2019 to provide a rehabilitative environment for inmates in the community to break the cycle of re-offending and help them successfully reintegrate into society.Karen Lee, Director of Community Corrections Command, shares the importance of aftercare support in an inmates rehabilitation journey. The programmes at SHWH aim to reiterate and reinforce key learning points from the pre-release programmes in prisons and focuses on the application in real-life situations such as work, studies and training. SPS adopts a Throughcare Approach in rehabilitation which supports the inmates journey from the time they come into prison (incare) to their pre-release stage and finally the time when they are released from prisons (aftercare).Lee shares, When they come into SHWH, there is a one-month intensive programme where they go through a refresher of the psychological-based correctional programme which they attended during their incare phase. At the same time, we also work with the Career Coaches from the Yellow Ribbon Singapore for job matching for the residents and send them for courses outside the SHWH.Besides inmates taking ownership and having the desire to change, successful reintegration also depends on other factors such as support from families, community partners, and employers, and acceptance and second chances from the broader community. We work with the different agencies in providing the support that they require. Support from employers to give sufficient employment opportunities is important as well. Last but not least, we are also working with their families to welcome them back, concluded Lee.BeyondPrisonWalls SG4SecondChances

caption: The moment he got caught, he collapsed. He dreaded the fate that awaited him. That was in 2008. 13 years later, life seems a lot more hopeful for the 49-year-old student inmate Tim (pseudonym). Tim is one of the oldest student inmates in Prison School, located in Institution Tanah Merah 1. The jovial and chatty student-inmate who sat for his GCE A-Levels examinations scored Distinctions and straight As.Ive got a second-second chance at life. The first was when my capital punishment for drug offences was reduced to life imprisonment in 2012, and second was when I managed to resume my education behind bars in 2018.. said Tim, who scored 9 points after retaking his GCE O-levels exams two years ago.Today, despite being in prison, Im competing with my 19-year-old son, who is preparing to pursue a diploma after scoring a 3.9 GPA in his ITE course. The father-of-one admits that being in prison and through resuming his education behind bars has helped bridge the gap between the father and son duo. We have so many more things to talk about nowThe biggest irony is that only after coming here, at my age, Ive resumed my education, renewed my relationship with my family and found my faith once again. Rehabilitation Officer (RO) Mohd Faizal actually played an important role in that. On a day to day basis, he checks in on me, helps me where possible and actually facilitates to get my religious books for me when I need them.. Tim intends to further his learning and quest for knowledge behind prison walls and leaves us with a message for the young students on the outside, Dont focus on temporary enjoyment. Develop a passion for learning. Not just education, but learning. Change comes when you start to learn.. BehindPrisonWalls CaptainsOfLives

That was in 2008. 13 years later, life seems a lot more hopeful for the 49-year-old student inmate (pseudonym). He is one of the oldest student inmates in Prison School, located in Institution Tanah Merah 1. The jovial and chatty student-inmate who sat for his GCE A-Levels examinations scored Distinctions and straight As. "I've got a second chance at life. The first was when my capital punishment for drug offences was reduced to life imprisonment in 2012, and second was when I managed to resume my education behind bars in 2018," he shared, who scored 9 points after retaking his GCE O-levels exams two years ago. Today, despite being in prison, he is competing with his 19-year-old son, who is preparing to pursue a diploma after scoring a 3.9 GPA in his ITE course. The father-of-one admits that being in prison and through resuming his education behind bars has helped bridge the gap between the father and son duo. "We have so many more things to talk about now." The biggest irony is that only after coming here, at my age, he has resumed his education, renewed his relationship with his family and found his faith once again. Rehabilitation Officer (RO) actually played an important role in that. On a day-to-day basis, he checks in on me, helps me where possible and actually facilitates to get my religious books for me when I need them. He intends to further his learning and quest for knowledge behind prison walls and leaves us with a message for the young students on the outside, "Don't focus on temporary enjoyment. Develop a passion for learning. Not just education, but learning. Change comes when you start to learn." #BehindPrisonWalls #CaptainsOfLives

caption: I met my wife in NUS in the year 1991. She was my mentor at that time, so I used that as an excuse to get her contact number! I did not think that she liked me but one day, I mustered my courage and confessed my feelings for her as we were walking towards the library. What a day that was! The only relief I felt was that I was not punched or slapped! And thats how our story started. SUPT Shahlan Bin Rashid recalls while glancing at his wife, SUPT Hamisah Bte Tomari.Actually, I heard about him even before we met. He was the popular guy on campus - smart and talented! I belonged to the type of girls who would find talented boys intriguing especially one that could draw and write poems, SUPT Hamisah laughed.When asked what its like to work in the same organisation as your spouse, SUPT Hamisah revealed, We understand the demands of our jobs and have equally accepted that both of us would always want to be there for our teams. We must be grateful for the opportunities we have, no matter how tough it can be. SUPT Shahlan chips in, I once remember cancelling our family dinner at the last minute because I was activated to report to work. As she worked in the same organisation, she understood the demands of my job, including such unexpected activations. Before we wrapped up, we asked them if they had any special plans for Valentines Day this year, to which SUPT Shahlan quickly responded, I have a 12-hour shift. SUPT Hamisah interrupted, He must make it up to me the following week!Happy Valentines Day from CaptainsofLives!

caption: I am Mohammad Firdaus Abdul Aziz, a Personal Supervisor from the Reformative Training Centre at Institution Tanah Merah 1, and this is my story.My father was my inspiration since young so it didnt take long for me to decide what my ultimate career would be a prison officer.An occurrence with an ex-offender reinforced my decision. I remember being at a shopping mall with my fiance (now wife) once, and an ex-offender approached me shouting, Sergeant Firdaus! at the top of his voice! As he approached me, I recalled that I had submitted a disciplinary report against him for misconduct when he was in prison, and yet today he greeted me with a friendly smile. He thanked me for being firm with him as it helped him learn from his past mistakes. He went on to share that he is pursuing his education once again and is now working part-time to support himself. That truly shed light that my actions today could have a ripple effect on inmates tomorrow. Along the way, Ive learnt that despite their current circumstances, we must constantly support them and guide them towards the right path and believe that change will happen. Maybe not today or tomorrow, but our efforts will pay off some day eventually.CaptainsOfLives

caption: The last time she broke down was in 2016, when her lawyer advised that the time she was looking at for her crime was close to 20 years behind bars. Two days ago, Anna (pseudonym), 29, wiped away tears once again. But this time, it was different. She wiped away her tears while holding her GCE O-Level certificate displaying straight As. Anna emerged as the top inmate student! She then walked back to her cell and scribbled I did it! in her diary. Its been six years since Anna started her prison sentence, but it was in her fifth year that she decided to retake her exams. When asked why it took her time to finally decide to retake her exams, she shared that she wanted to be 100% certain that she would give it her best and not take the opportunity lightly. Opportunities like this dont come easy, I would never take it for granted. Since then, with pure grit and determination, she spent her days studying. Despite the circumstances that surround Anna, today is a good day. One should recognise their own capabilities and have more faith. Believe you can do it, shared Anna. Inmate students like Anna, learn discipline, focus and gradually begin to trust their own abilities and skills when they resume their education behind bars. Anna credits her results to her Math and Form Teacher Ms Jayme, She treated me like an adult student. She pushed me and encouraged me every step of the way.When asked about her dreams, Annas eyes reflect a sense of longing. She shared that she often dreams of the time when she is released and takes the train home, I imagine myself standing at the platform and taking the train back from the East all the way to Jurong and simply watching people.. It might be a while till she rides the train back home, but today, as she gets up to leave, we notice a little bounce in her step. Walking us out, Rehabilitation Officer (RO) Lee Sang Hui shared how despite the length of her sentence, Anna remains focused and knows what she wants to achieve in her time in prison. Anna is currently serving a 22-year sentence for drug offences. She has chosen to sit for her GCE A-Level examinations later this year. CaptainsOfLives BehindPrisonWalls

caption: Having played truant since young, Ahmad (pseudonym) was expelled from school in Secondary 2. His teenage years were spent in the chaos of drugs and gangs. However, the pitfalls of his youth are a faint representation of the Ahmad today, aged 36, an inmate student who was one of the top performers in Prison School, located in Institution Tanah Merah 1, with all As and a B for his GCE O-Level 2020. I never thought I would be able to hold an O-Level certificate. This is long overdue., an overwhelmed Ahmad expressed. He shared that at his age, he thought it was an impossible dream. However, his English teacher, Mr Lim Jien Hwee, was not surprised with Ahmads performance as he had always worked hard and was consistent in his performance throughout last year. Having served five years of his thirteen-year sentence, Ahmad shared that he wanted his time behind bars to be spent meaningfully and resuming his education will secure a brighter future for him after release. When asked about his daily routine and what kept him motivated to study, Ahmad credited his desire to make his family and his children, an 8-year-old son and 13-year-old step-daughter, proud. He also fondly recalled how Chief Warder (CW) Mohammad Rashids encouragement helped him. He pointed us to CW Mohammad Rashid across the auditorium with a thumbs up and shared that CW Rashid always mentored him.Education is a stepping-stone for inmates like Ahmad to build a better future for themselves when they are released back to the society. Importantly, inmate students also pick up positive values and soft skills, such as resilience, perseverance, stress management and time management, which are important values and skills to have when they are released from the prisons.Looking forward, Ahmad remembered his childhood dream of being a Software Engineer. But before that, he will take his GCE A-Level examination first. CaptainsOfLives BehindPrisonWalls

caption: After going through the H.E.R.O programme, I can now recognise my hidden emotions and manage them in positive ways. It has also helped me to build positive thoughts whenever I face problems. Fai (pseudonym) Honour, Empathy, Resilience, Ownership (H.E.R.O) is a 6-month needs based violence intervention programme led by psychologists from the Singapore Prison Service (SPS) for inmates with violent offence(s). H.E.R.O aims to target cognitions related to violence as well as equip inmates with relevant skills and behavioural strategies to refrain from violence. Personal development activities are also scheduled for inmates to help them develop a repertoire of prosocial interests and hobbies to use their time productively.Since 2019, SPS has partnered Singapore to engage inmates productively through sport activities, led by ActiveSG Sport Champions.Sports is for all! Beyond promoting an active lifestyle, ActiveSG hopes that the introduction of sporting activities can help our inmates to manage their emotions better, reflect on life lessons and build confidence in themselves. With the success of this pilot project, it strengthens our resolve to scale this programme to benefit more inmates together with SPS. - Chief ActiveSG, Sng Hock Lin.

caption: 2020 marks the largest number of inmate participants to receive the highest honour in the National Youth Achievement Awards (NYAA) - with 80 inmates achieving the NYAA Gold!The NYAA programme was first introduced in prison for inmates aged between 16 and 25. This year, the age limit was increased to 30, which allowed more inmates to benefit from the programme. To date, more than 2,000 inmates have completed the NYAA programme since it started in Singapore Prison Service in 2000. In line with this years theme, Dare Explore, Lead ur Dreams, Strive Succeed, Better ur Society, NYAA participants initiated several outreach projects even in the midst of the nations battle against the COVID-19 pandemic. This included developing posters about the virus that were used within prison institutions to educate fellow inmates, and making personalised cards to show their appreciation to our frontline healthcare workers. President Halimah Yacob, who was the Guest-of-Honour for this years ceremony, also commended the awardees in her speech.This year marks a key milestone, as we celebrate the 20th year of NYAA in Prisons. To each one of you who embarked on your NYAA journey, you can be proud of yourselves for making this effort to change. Your NYAA experience will stand you in good stead, and I look forward to you making a positive impact on our society. BehindPrisonWalls

caption: Children's Day is a day held dear for children and families. For some of them, they might be unable to celebrate this special day and other milestones together with their loved ones.This year, Singapore Prison Service worked with The Salvation Army Prison Support Services - Kids In Play (TSA PSS-KIP) and the Centre for Fathering (CFF) to organise six virtual bonding sessions over Zoom in October and November, where inmates were able to bond and engage in fun game activities together with their children. witnessed one of the sessions organised by CFF at the Prison Link Centre on 5 November 2020 and shared his hopes for the inmates to change for the better and return to a strong, supportive family.Family support programmes are crucial in enhancing familial relationships and reducing the impact of incarceration on family members, especially children of those incarcerated. By working closely with community partners to provide these programmes, we aim to bridge the gap between the inmates and their children and strengthen their family ties. This will go a long way in their reintegration process when they are released eventually.CaptainsOfLives

caption: Did you know that the YR Industries (YRI) Laundry Workshop at Institution A5 is one of the largest health care laundries in Southeast Asia? Its also the biggest workshop in Changi Prison Complex (CPC)! In our conversation with SCW James Lew, who is also the Team Leader at the laundry workshop, we found out how he supports inmates and finds time to meaningfully engage them during working hours. He shares, I maximise the small pockets of time during the inmates tea break and find opportunity to engage inmates in spontaneous conversations. These small blocks of interactions help me cultivate relationships with them over time. To me, this is a subtle but effective way of engagement with inmates to influence them positively and encourage them to turn over a new leaf.Besides ensuring that the operations are running smoothly and efficiently in the Laundry Workshop, SCW James Lew and his team have a bigger role inspiring the inmates at every chance towards a society without re-offending! CaptainsOfLives
cleaned_caption: Did you know that the YR Industries (YRI) Laundry Workshop at Institution A5 is one of the largest health care laundries in Southeast Asia? It's also the biggest workshop in Changi Prison Complex (CPC)! In our conversation with the Team Leader at the laundry workshop, we found out how he supports inmates and finds time to meaningfully engage them during working hours. He shares, "I maximise the small pockets of time during the inmates' tea break and find opportunity to engage inmates in spontaneous conversations. These small blocks of interactions help me cultivate relationships with them over time. To me, this is a subtle but effective way of engagement with inmates to influence them positively and encourage them to turn over a new leaf." Besides ensuring that the operations are running smoothly and efficiently in the Laundry Workshop, he and his team have a bigger role inspiring the inmates at every chance towards a society without re-offending! CaptainsOfLives

caption: Amiable and adventurous with a quick wit and rollicking sense of humour, Rehabilitation Officer (RO) Sherman Kwang is always busy; and even more so during the past few months since he was casted as the main lead for Captains of Lives (COL) recently debuted short film, The Test. With a passion to make a difference to society, he pursued a degree in Criminology and Security at the Singapore Institute of Technology and joined the Singapore Prison Service as a Prison Officer after his graduation.Going beyond just supervising and creating a safe environment for inmates in Changi Prison Complex, RO Sherman and his fellow colleagues act as role models for them during their daily interactions. When inmates display negative emotions, instead of getting angry at them, we show them how to control their own emotions and handle such situations with patience, said RO Sherman, who plays a vital role in the rehabilitation of the inmates as a Housing Unit Officer in Institution A1. No aspirations shared by the inmates is ever taken lightly, We take their requests seriously and find every opportunity to refer them to the right avenues to increase their chance of having a successful reintegration into society."Going the extra mile to engage each inmate individually, RO Sherman shared with us the importance of doing so. Only through individual engagement without any other inmates or staff around will they feel more comfortable to talk naturally and bare their hearts out. When asked about his proudest moment as a CaptainsofLives, he recalled the time when an inmate who was about to be released approached him to thank him for bringing him comfort whenever he needed a listening ear. He made me realise that as a prison officer, sometimes, all we need to provide is a listening ear, and ask them how they feel. Wondering how Captains Of Lives like Sherman make a difference in the lives of those serving their sentences? Watch the latest short film The Test by Singapore Prison Service on our IGTV!

caption: Meet Chief Warder Joann and Chief Warder Cleve she's a Senior Personal Supervisor at Institution A4 and he's a Team Leader at the Singapore Prisons Emergency Action Response (SPEAR) Force. Over a decade ago, they bumped into each other in a lift at the same HDB block they live in and today, theyve been happily married for 9 years!When asked about how its like working together within the Singapore Prison Service, Joann said, I think its great because not many people understand the line of work we do and to have someone who understands what I'm going through I am grateful for that, as we are able to share and learn from each other." Even though their paths crossed at work, Cleve says, "We are able to remain professional and deconflict. We do not mix work and personal matters when we are together." Akin to any other couples, ValentinesDay celebrations are a yearly ritual! This year, a simple dinner date after work is in the works for this couple, as they believe that the company is what matters! We cant help but agree whether your significant other is from work or elsewhere, have a Happy Valentines' Day!

caption: It was October 3rd when alarms were blasting the entire Institution B1 of Changi Prison Complex. A group of "rioters" had damaged and taken control of a prison workshop. Almost immediately, a team of prison officers were assembled to manage the situation while another team situated themselves in the Institution Operations Room, plotting plans to prevent the "riot" from escalating further. A eight-man team from the Singapore Prisons Emergency Action Response (SPEAR) Force responded and swiftly quelled the mock prison riot by subduing the "rioters" with non-lethal pistols and launchers that fire pellets. In a matter of seconds, all the "rioters" were subdued. SUPT Ng Kim Seng, Assistant Commander (Operations) for Cluster B, said: "Riot is considered a major incident in prison because it can lead to a high number of casualties, and inmates would start to damage our prison properties, which would threaten the secure custody of the prisoners," "When a riot happens, the ground officers will try to minimise casualties and manage the incident to the best of their abilities. If the situation goes out of control, they will escalate upwards to seek support from other units such as SPEAR and K9 to come in and help quell the riot." BehindPrisonWalls

caption: As part of this years National Day Parade (NDP ) celebrations, Reach Out gave inmates the chance to pack the NDP funpacks. It is one of the community service initiatives undertaken by inmates, allowing them to participate in meaningful activities that benefit the community.Senior Chief Warder 1 Illamaran Thamizhkkothai, Chief Personal Supervisor at Institution B5 of Changi Prison Complex, oversees the inmate volunteers who are helping to pack the NDP funpacks. The inmates here feel very proud and honoured to take part in this funpacking. They feel that they are part of Singapore [and by] doing this for the people and the country, it is very meaningful for them, said SCW Thamizhkkothai. She also shared that the inmate volunteers are generally in high spirits and very enthusiastic during their shifts, as they are motivated to hit their goal of packing 50,000 funpacks by July. CaptainsOfLives BehindPrisonWalls NDP2019 OurSingapore ALittleMoreSG

caption: The Singapore Prison Service (SPS) works with various religious organisations such as Fitrah - Family and Inmates ThRoughcare Assistance Haven (FITRAH) to support Muslim inmates and ex-offenders in their rehabilitation and reintegration journey. "FITRAH is something which is new [and spearheaded] by the Islamic Religious Council of Singapore (MUIS) its a coordinating body that work with other community partners within the Malay-Muslim organisations like MENDAKI Singapore , People's Association Malay Activity Executive Committees Council (MESRA) to provide weekly Muslim religious programmes and services for inmates in incare, said SUPT Faisal Mustaffa, Superintendent at Institution B4 of Changi Prison. Additionally, FITRAH offers aftercare assistance BeyondPrisonWalls by reaching out to ex-offenders upon their release at the prison gate and issuing a Back-To-FITRAH package that helps ex-offenders begin a new life. This is symbolic of a new start in their lives and the beginning of a journey of reintegration into society.

caption: [3/4] OBSERVING RAMADAN BEHINDPRISONWALLS: Ramadan is known to be a month of fasting, prayer and reflection for Muslims. We asked Rehabilitation Officer (RO) Mohamed Faizal Bin Abdul Hamid what changes he observed in the inmates under his charge. During this time, we can see an increase in their religious obligations like reading the Quran and performing terawih (non-obligatory prayers) in their cells," said RO Faizal. Inmates can also attend weekly religious classes and prayer sessions conducted by volunteers. By interacting with these volunteers, inmates are introduced to pro-social networks which they can depend on upon their release. During conversations with the inmates under my charge, I can sense their regret and resolve to ensure that this will be their last Ramadan in prison, as they reflect on how their actions have led to them being taken away from their families." CaptainsOfLives Photo: Muhamad Darwisy Haroon

caption: [2/4] OBSERVING RAMADAN BEHINDPRISONWALLS: We spoke to Rehabilitation Officer (RO) Mohamed Faizal Bin Abdul Hamid on the challenges of keeping Singapore safe and secure, while fasting. One of the obvious challenges during Ramadan is not being able to eat and drink while performing our duties, said RO Faizal. "Our shifts last up to 12 hours, during which, we are required to be vigilant to keep the housing units safe and secure." During Ramadan, there are additional operations to help facilitate inmates who are observing the holy month. For example, some staff will be detailed beyond their working hours to help supervise the issuance of medication. Some daily routines are changed to accommodate the Muslim inmates who are fasting," explains RO Faizal. "The lights and water are turned on earlier and turned off later so that inmates can have their pre-dawn meals and prayers. The kitchen staff also have to start prepping dinner earlier for Muslim inmates who are breaking fast in the evening. CaptainsOfLives Photo: Muhamad Darwisy Haroon
cleaned_caption: [2/4] OBSERVING RAMADAN BEHIND PRISON WALLS: We spoke to Rehabilitation Officer (RO) on the challenges of keeping Singapore safe and secure, while fasting. One of the obvious challenges during Ramadan is not being able to eat and drink while performing our duties, said RO. "Our shifts last up to 12 hours, during which, we are required to be vigilant to keep the housing units safe and secure." During Ramadan, there are additional operations to help facilitate inmates who are observing the holy month. For example, some staff will be detailed beyond their working hours to help supervise the issuance of medication. Some daily routines are changed to accommodate the Muslim inmates who are fasting," explains RO. "The lights and water are turned on earlier and turned off later so that inmates can have their pre-dawn meals and prayers. The kitchen staff also have to start prepping dinner earlier for Muslim inmates who are breaking fast in the evening. CaptainsOfLives Photo:

BehindPrisonWalls

caption: Hey there, my name is ASP Venu Arsh and I am a trainer in SPEAR Force and I oversee tactical re-certification for HQ officers and SPEAR Tactical Course. I also help to train our prison officers to make tactical decisions and operational judgement in different environment in either Cluster or external settings. caption: To provide greater aftercare support for ex-offenders, the Selarang Halfway House is the first Government-run facility to provide structured aftercare support for selected ex-offenders who are emplaced on the Mandatory Aftercare Scheme.The newly operationalised facility is designed to provide a conducive rehabilitative environment for ex-offenders after their release from prison. They will attend counselling sessions and are allowed to work or attend vocational training during their stay there. Staff from Selarang Halfway House will work with the residents to address their reintegration challenges, adopt more prosocial behaviours and facilitate their progress in the reintegration journey. Through these efforts, we hope to help them break away from their cycle of re-offending and continue living crime-free lives in the community. CaptainsOfLives BehindPrisonWalls BeyondPrisonWalls

caption: "Volunteers are a positive source of influence that inmates and ex-offenders can turn to upon their release or if they need help," said Superintendent (SUPT) Valerie Chiang, who is also the Senior Assistant Director of the Community & Family Policy Branch.She shared that volunteers come from all walks of life including secular and religious groups, and play an important role in supporting our initiatives and rehabilitation. "Volunteers really value-add to the rehabilitative efforts that SPS is putting in as pro-social support is something that is very important and isn't readily available. Community partners such as Volunteer Welfare Organisations and SPS befrienders offer a source of positive influence that these inmates can turn to upon their release. By meeting people who can journey with them, this positive influence can lead inmates to the path of the straight and narrow. " she said. While we believe that inmates need to take charge of their own rehabilitation, prison volunteers and community partners also play a vital role in supporting inmates rehabilitation and reintegration into society. This helps us in our vision of a society without re-offending.CaptainsofLives BeyondPrisonWalls

caption: PART 4/4: SPEAR SelectionThe end is near when the candidates moved on to the final stage of the SPEAR selection, the panel interview. Make it or break it? This is their last straw.The 10 candidates were all gathered in a room, while they catch their breath and prepare for the final call. Their heartbeat got faster each time the SPEAR trainer walks into the room and calls out the name of a candidate to attend the panel interview.We took the time to speak to then-SPEAR Commander, Ong Aik San, on what traits do the Elite force look out for in a SPEAR Officer. The candidate has to be strong and disciplined. After they finish the initial training to be a SPEAR officer, it is up to them to maintain their fitness and tactical level. captainsoflives behindprisonwalls

caption: In this season of giving, nothing can be more precious than giving the family members of the Performing Arts Centre (PAC) inmates the chance to catch them live in concert.After the performance, they handed over a handmade card filled with their thoughts to their family members and were treated to an open visit, where they could hug and talk to their family members. While some shared their deepest remorse with their parents, others were delighted to hear about new additions in their family.As Captains of Lives, we recognise that family support is crucial in the rehabilitation journey of inmates. By providing targeted rehabilitation along with family support, we can ensure the seamless reintegration of ex-offenders into society. captainsoflives behindprisonwalls

caption: To tackle the over-representation in crime and drug statistics of the Malay-Muslim community, the Development and Reintegration Programme for Malay-Muslim inmates was developed by the Singapore Prison Service in collaboration with the Association of Muslim Professionals (AMP). The programme provides interventions for Malay-Muslim inmates in the Drug Rehabilitation Centres and their families, for a period of up to 12 months after the inmates' release. Despite not having experience in aftercare work, AMP rose to the challenge by recruiting and training case workers to work closely with inmates and their families to address their reintegration needs.AMP spokesperson, Mdm Hameet Khanee J Hussain, a senior manager at AMP who heads the social services division, said: One of AMPs core focuses is to empower the underprivileged and help them to take charge of their lives. By offering a holistic intervention and addressing the concerns the inmate has while he is incarcerated, he can then focus on his rehabilitation and reintegration journey. The Malay-Muslim population in prison is disproportionally high and we must, as a community, come forward to lead and to support them in their reintegration and rehabilitation needsBeyondPrisonWalls CaptainsOfLives

The Malay-Muslim population in prison is disproportionally high and we must, as a community, come forward to lead and to support them in their reintegration and rehabilitation needs. BeyondPrisonWalls CaptainsOfLives

caption: The Yellow Ribbon Conference (YRC) was held recently, with the aim of providing professionals in corrections a shared platform to share best practices and discuss upstream prevention, rehabilitation, and reintegration of inmates and ex-offenders. Themed Creating Ripples of Change at Every Level, topics such as Youth Criminal Conduct and Substance Abuse Treatment, Families and Incarceration, and Offender Rehabilitation and Community Corrections will be discussed by a variety of industry professionals at the conference. These include psychologists and Correctional Rehabilitation Specialists (CRS) from the Singapore Prison Service, the Central Narcotics Bureau, Ministry of Social and Family Development, as well as aftercare professionals from Singapore Anti-Narcotics Association and Trybe. We spoke to Dr Gabriel Ong, Chairperson, YR Conference and Principal Psychologist with the Singapore Prison Service. Dr Ong shared that he would assess the risk of inmates and recommend suitable programmes to change their attitude. The inmates will then be able to weigh the pros and cons of their actions and this will increase their motivation to change.Dr Ong emphasized on the importance of employer and community support in the rehabilitation journey of ex-offenders. The ex-offenders need to practice the skills they acquired in prison in a natural environment, for instance, their workplace, Dr Gabriel Ong, Chairperson, YR Conference.When asked what he hoped to achieve from this conference, Dr Ong mentioned that he hopes to be inspired by the best practices of the world and share the Singapore Prison Services correctional policy with leaders all over the world.CaptainsofLives BeyondPrisonWalls

Captains of Lives Beyond Prison Walls.

caption: President Halimah Yacob visited the Changi Prison Complex this morning and warmly engaged with women inmates from Institution A4. As the only womens prison, Institution A4 offers a wide range of rehabilitation programmes catered to address the unique risks and needs of women inmates.President Halimah Yacob was given a tour of the various vocational training facilities such as the hairdressing workshop and bakery to see how we work closely with SCORE to enhance the employability of inmates. She also visited a classroom where a counselling session was taking place to see how SPSs team of Psychologists and Correctional Rehabilitation Specialists work closely with prison officers to conduct evidence-based psychological and counselling programmes for these inmates. Thank you Madam President for taking time to visit us today and for inspiring our inmates to take charge of their own reintegration journeys. BehindPrisonWalls CaptainsOfLives

caption: Technology empowers inmates to take charge and be responsible for their own rehabilitation an important mindset change needed for them to successfully reintegrate into society when they leave prison. For example, they can purchase canteen items from a vending machine automatically, and also engage in self-learning on tablets. The test-bedding of technology in A4 has also allowed our prison officers to be more than just guards. They can be Captains of Lives mentors, coaches and role models to inmates, guiding them to become contributing members of society when they leave prison. says SUPT 1A Angeline Chua of Institution A4, which houses over 1,000 female inmates. As the Second Superintendent, SUPT Angeline oversees the smooth running of operations at Institution A4.Institution A4 has demonstrated how technology can transform the way our prisons are run. A key benefit of introducing these technologies would be to improve the work of a prison officer many routine and manual processes would be eradicated, enabling the officers to focus on purposeful interactions to better manage the inmates and facilitate their rehabilitation.SUPT Angeline continues to feel inspired as a Captain of Lives and she continues to be driven by the motivation of making an impact on the lives of inmates and their families. BehindPrisonWalls CaptainsOfLives

caption: Parliamentary Secretary for Home Affairs and Health, Mr Amrin Amin and Commissioner of Prisons, Mr Desmond Chin, hosted Minister for Culture, Community and Youth (MCCY), Ms Grace Fu, Parliamentary Secretary for MCCY, Mr Baey Yam Keng and other National Youth Council (NYC) members at the Community Rehabilitation Centre (CRC) yesterday as part of a learning journey.The CRC, which is managed by a non-profit organisation Trybe, provides a transformational environment for young 1st time drug offenders to take charge of their own rehabilitation and reintegration goals. Our guests learnt about the structured residential programme which allows the youth to continue with their education and employment in the day, ensuring minimal disruption to their lives. They also met with two residents who shared their stories of how they were empowered to turn their lives around.As Captains of Lives, we are committed to providing offenders with the support for them to take charge of their own rehabilitation and reintegration journey. While these offenders are capable of change and can contribute to society, we believe that family and community support is critical too for their rehabilitation. BeyondPrisonWalls

caption: About 145 inmates aged 25 and below from Tanah Merah Prison (TMP) and Changi Womens Prison (CWP) received the National Youth Achievement Awards (NYAA) yesterday the highest number of inmate participants since the introduction of the programme. Guest of honour, Parliamentary Secretary for Home Affairs Amrin Amin, congratulated award winners at the event yesterday, adding: No matter what your personal circumstances may be, with positive goals, determination and the right attitude, you can accomplish many things in life. More than 1,600 inmates have participated in the NYAA programme since the Singapore Prison Service has implemented it in 2000. The programme aims to encourage young inmates to develop personal qualities of self-reliance, perseverance and a sense of responsibility not only to themselves but also to the community. Through embarking on the different components that were held within CWP and TMP, participants are able to gain greater awareness of their roles in society and learn how their skills, talent and knowledge can contribute towards the community. Kudos to our NYAA award recipients this year who have done us proud! BehindPrisonWalls

caption: The Singapore Prison Service (SPS) today outlined its strategic roadmap for 2017, focusing on Transformation through technology, data-driven operatio ns coupled with community corrections at the annual SPS-SCORE (Singapore Corporation of Rehabilitative Enterprises) Corporate Advance. The workplan was graced by Guest-of-Honour, Minister for Home Affairs and Minister for Law, K Shanmugam. With the theme Deepening Collaborations, Realising Transformation, SPS will continue expanding its role as a correctional agency which ensures the safe and secure custody of inmates and the rehabilitation and reintegration of ex-offenders. To build a future-ready correctional service, SPS embarked on the concept of Prisons Without Guards, where it will leverage technology to enhance operational effectiveness and automate work processes. Community partnership also remains essential in supporting rehabilitation and reintegration. SPS is working with the Association of Muslim Professionals (AMP) which will be piloting the first integrated throughcare programme for Muslim drug abusers this year. Inmates will be provided with Personal Development Programmes while they are in prison and followed through by AMPs case workers. Families of inmates can also enrol in AMPs Family and Youth Development Programme which aims to help them become financially stable and economically dependent. SPS and SCORE will continue to engage and work closely with our community partners and volunteers to support the inmates, ex-offenders and their families to face the challenges of the future.

caption: Performance reviews are conducted regularly to ensure that Singapore Prison Service (SPS) officers remain competent in their daily work deliverables and to evaluate if they are ready to take on higher roles and responsibilities.Similarly, Deputy Superintendent (DSP) Ng Kailin has recently been promoted as Head Curriculum and Planning, which came as a pleasant surprise for her as she had just returned from her maternity leave. Having previously led a ground operation unit in managing offenders in a Drug Rehabilitation Centre (DRC), DSP Ngs work revolved around ensuring the smooth operations within the housing unit as well as maintaining a high level of security and discipline, while overseeing the rehabilitation and reintegration needs of the offenders under her care. By excelling in her given roles and responsibilities at work, and through the support of her management, DSP Ng was promoted and given the chance to better hone her leadership skills.Today, she leads an entire staff unit in the planning of training curriculum for prison officers to better equip them for the challenges that they might face at work.With all the good work that she has done, DSP Ng has not only proven to be successful at work, but at home as well, as a devoted wife and a mother of one with another one on the way.

caption: Last month, the Singapore Prison Service (SPS) paid tribute to its Pioneer Prison Officers for their invaluable contributions in keeping Singapore safe and secure over the past few decades. Of the 184 Pioneers who attended the SPS Pioneers Appreciation Dinner 2015, we had the opportunity to speak with several of them, including Mr Suppiah Mahalingam (pictured left) and Mr Ekhuan Bin Sawal (right). When asked what it takes for someone to be a true, blue Captain of Lives, Mr Mahalingam highlighted the importance of self-confidence when carrying out the various tasks, as well as the importance of personal development through education and training. Mr Ekhuan added that it was highly necessary that prison officers remain firm, yet fair in their daily interaction with inmates, as well as the importance of proper planning before each task is being carried out.Through exemplifying integrity, resilience and strength, these SPS Pioneers have left a legacy for current and future generations of prison officers to mirror in times to come.COL50Things

caption: During the Yellow Ribbon Prison (YRP) Run 2015, we caught up with several Singapore Prison Service Officers to find out more about their involvement during the significant event.One of the officers we met was Rehabilitation Officer (RO), Ahmad Sophan Bin Basir Ahmad, who was placed in charge of the Rehabilitation Exhibition located at the carnival.As a firm believer of the Yellow Ribbon Project, RO Sophan shared that this was the second year of his involvement in the YRP Run and added that he enjoys how the community comes together to pave the way for acceptance during the annual run. Sophan also recounted some of his personal encounters with ex-offenders who were able to change for the better, through the encouragement and support of the community and the people around them. He highlighted how through his encouragement and advice to an ex-offender to focus on learning, instead of pressurizing himself to change, the ex-offender was able to take practical actions in his daily life, which has in turn enabled him to be successful in life, having fostered strong relations with his family and is a proud owner of a cupcake business.Stay tuned to this page as we bring to you more features of officers who were involved.

caption: As part of the SG50 celebrations, we interviewed our Pioneer Prison Officers to better understand how the Singapore Prison Service (SPS) has evolved into the correctional agency that it is today, which focuses not just on the rehabilitation of offenders, but enforces their secure custody as well.Having joined the service in 1972, Mr. Leo Teng Jit served SPS for over 33 years before retiring in 2006.During his course of work, Mr. Leo has seen how SPS has progressed over the years, with the enhancement of the Changi Prison Complex infrastructure, which ensures the security of the prison compound. Mr. Leo also noted that the quality of the rehabilitation programmes and community engagements and volunteers have increased tremendously in line with the evolving needs of the inmates.Mr. Leo believes that development should not stop here, and encourages present-day prison officers to keep abreast of the changes in the correctional sector and perform their very best in the duties laid before them. Join us in commemorating the efforts taken by our Pioneers who have helped built Singapore to what it is today, a safe country for all!WhenWeWereYoung

During his course of work, he has seen how SPS has progressed over the years, with the enhancement of the Changi Prison Complex infrastructure, which ensures the security of the prison compound. He also noted that the quality of the rehabilitation programmes and community engagements and volunteers have increased tremendously in line with the evolving needs of the inmates. He believes that development should not stop here, and encourages present-day prison officers to keep abreast of the changes in the correctional sector and perform their very best in the duties laid before them. Join us in commemorating the efforts taken by our Pioneers who have helped build Singapore to what it is today, a safe country for all!#WhenWeWereYoung

caption: The National Youth Achievement Awards (NYAA) Presentation Ceremony will be held on Thursday, 23rd April 2015 at the Tanah Merah Prison. Since the implementation of the NYAA programme in the year 2000, more than 1460 awards have been given out to inmates between the ages of 18 and 25. This year, a total of 96 inmates qualified for the NYAA awards, after having completed several non-competitive activities, which were centered on helping them develop personal qualities of self-reliance, perseverance and a sense of responsibility not only to themselves, but to society and the nation as well.The Singapore Prison Service is committed to providing inmates with the support and opportunities needed for them to rehabilitate, so that they can break out from the cycle of re-offending and be contributors to society. By successfully impacting change, we are able to create a safer nation for all.
